question: 5. A circle of radius \( a \) is revolved through \( 180^{\circ} \) about a line in its plane, distant \( b \) from the center of the circle, where \( b>a \). For what value of the ratio \( b / a \) does the center of gravity of the solid thus generated lie on the surface of the solid?
solution:
Solution. We choose axes so that the generating circle starts in the \( x-z \) plane and is revolved about the \( z \)-axis. The generated solid is half of a toroid (i.e., a solid bounded by a torus).

It is clear from symmetry that the centroid lies at a point \( (0, \bar{y}, 0) \) on the \( y \)-axis, and the requirement of the problem is that \( \bar{y}=b-a \).

To find the centroid we introduce polar coordinates in the \( x-y \) plane. Corresponding to the element of area \( r d r d \theta \) in the plane there is the element of volume
\[
2 \sqrt{a^{2}-(r-b)^{2}} r d r d \theta
\]
which contributes
\[
2 r \sin \theta \sqrt{a^{2}-(r-b)^{2}} r d r d \theta
\]
to the moment \( M_{y} \) of the solid in the \( y \) direction: We have \( \bar{y}=M_{y} / V \) where \( V \) is the volume of the semi-toroid.
\[
\begin{aligned}
V & =\int_{0}^{\pi} \int_{b-a}^{b+a} 2 \sqrt{a^{2}}-\overline{(r-b)^{2}} r d r d \theta \\
& =2 \pi \int_{b-a}^{b+a} \sqrt{a^{2}-(r-\bar{b})^{2}} r d r \\
& =2 \pi a^{2} \int_{-\pi / 2}^{\pi / 2} \cos \phi(b+a \sin \phi) \cos \phi d \phi \\
& =\pi^{2} a^{2} b . \\
M_{y} & =\int_{0}^{\pi} \int_{b-a}^{b+a} 2 \sqrt{a^{2}-(r-b)^{2}} r^{2} d r \sin \theta d \theta \\
& =4 \int_{b-a}^{b+a} \sqrt{a^{2}-\frac{1}{(r-b)^{2}} r^{2} d r} \\
& =4 a^{2} \int_{-\pi / 2}^{\pi / 2} \cos \phi(b+a \sin \phi)^{2} \cos \phi d \phi \\
& =2 \pi a^{2} b^{2}+\frac{\pi}{2} a^{4} .
\end{aligned}
\]

In both integrals we used the substitution \( r=b+a \sin \phi \).
Hence
\[
\bar{y}=\frac{M_{y}}{V}=\frac{a^{2}+4 b^{2}}{2 \pi b} .
\]

But we require \( \bar{y}=b-a \), so
\[
2 \pi b^{2}-2 \pi a b=a^{2}+4 b^{2} .
\]

If \( c=b / a \), then
\[
(2 \pi-4) c^{2}-2 \pi c-1=0
\]
and
\[
c=\frac{\pi+\sqrt{\pi^{2}+2 \pi-4}}{2 \pi-4} .
\]

We chose the positive sign since \( \boldsymbol{c} \) must be positive.
Remarks. The volume of the semi-toroid could have been obtained from Pappus's Theorem that the volume of a solid of revolution is the product of the area of the generating region times the length of the circle traversed by the centroid of the area.

The critical ratio is about 2.91 .

descriptive_long.question: 5. A circle of radius \( radius \) is revolved through \( 180^{\circ} \) about a line in its plane, distant \( axisdist \) from the center of the circle, where \( axisdist>radius \). For what value of the ratio \( axisdist / radius \) does the center of gravity of the solid thus generated lie on the surface of the solid?
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. We choose axes so that the generating circle starts in the \( xcoord-zcoord \) plane and is revolved about the \( zcoord \)-axis. The generated solid is half of a toroid (i.e., a solid bounded by a torus).

It is clear from symmetry that the centroid lies at a point \( (0, \bar{ycoord}, 0) \) on the \( ycoord \)-axis, and the requirement of the problem is that \( \bar{ycoord}=axisdist-radius \).

To find the centroid we introduce polar coordinates in the \( xcoord-ycoord \) plane. Corresponding to the element of area \( radial \, d radial \, d angletheta \) in the plane there is the element of volume
\[
2 \sqrt{radius^{2}-(radial-axisdist)^{2}} \, radial \, d radial \, d angletheta
\]
which contributes
\[
2 \, radial \sin angletheta \, \sqrt{radius^{2}-(radial-axisdist)^{2}} \, radial \, d radial \, d angletheta
\]
to the moment \( momenty \) of the solid in the \( ycoord \) direction. We have \( \bar{ycoord}=momenty / volume \) where \( volume \) is the volume of the semi-toroid.
\[
\begin{aligned}
volume & = \int_{0}^{\pi} \int_{axisdist-radius}^{axisdist+radius} 2 \sqrt{radius^{2}}-\overline{(radial-axisdist)^{2}} \, radial \, d radial \, d angletheta \\
       & = 2\pi \int_{axisdist-radius}^{axisdist+radius} \sqrt{radius^{2}-(radial-\bar{axisdist})^{2}} \, radial \, d radial \\
       & = 2\pi \, radius^{2} \int_{-\pi/2}^{\pi/2} \cos anglephi \, (axisdist+radius \sin anglephi) \cos anglephi \, d anglephi \\
       & = \pi^{2} \, radius^{2} \, axisdist . \\
momenty & = \int_{0}^{\pi} \int_{axisdist-radius}^{axisdist+radius} 2 \sqrt{radius^{2}-(radial-axisdist)^{2}} \, radial^{2} \, d radial \sin angletheta \, d angletheta \\
        & = 4 \int_{axisdist-radius}^{axisdist+radius} \sqrt{radius^{2}-\frac{1}{(radial-axisdist)^{2}}} \, radial^{2} \, d radial \\
        & = 4 \, radius^{2} \int_{-\pi/2}^{\pi/2} \cos anglephi \, (axisdist+radius \sin anglephi)^{2} \cos anglephi \, d anglephi \\
        & = 2\pi \, radius^{2} \, axisdist^{2} + \frac{\pi}{2} radius^{4} .
\end{aligned}
\]

In both integrals we used the substitution \( radial = axisdist + radius \sin anglephi \).
Hence
\[
\bar{ycoord}=\frac{momenty}{volume}=\frac{radius^{2}+4 \, axisdist^{2}}{2\pi \, axisdist} .
\]
But we require \( \bar{ycoord}=axisdist-radius \), so
\[
2\pi \, axisdist^{2} - 2\pi \, radius \, axisdist = radius^{2} + 4 \, axisdist^{2} .
\]
If \( ratiofactor = axisdist / radius \), then
\[
(2\pi-4) \, ratiofactor^{2} - 2\pi \, ratiofactor - 1 = 0
\]
and
\[
ratiofactor = \frac{\pi + \sqrt{\pi^{2}+2\pi-4}}{2\pi-4} .
\]
We choose the positive sign since \( \boldsymbol{ratiofactor} \) must be positive.

Remarks. The volume of the semi-toroid could have been obtained from Pappus's theorem that the volume of a solid of revolution is the product of the area of the generating region and the length of the circle traversed by the centroid of the area.

The critical ratio is about 2.91 .

descriptive_long_confusing.question: A circle of radius \( waterlily \) is revolved through \( 180^{\circ} \) about a line in its plane, distant \( rainstorm \) from the center of the circle, where \( rainstorm>waterlily \). For what value of the ratio \( rainstorm / waterlily \) does the center of gravity of the solid thus generated lie on the surface of the solid?
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. We choose axes so that the generating circle starts in the \( lemonade\text{-}pancakes \) plane and is revolved about the \( pancakes \)-axis. The generated solid is half of a toroid (i.e., a solid bounded by a torus).

It is clear from symmetry that the centroid lies at a point \( (0, \bar{orchestra}, 0) \) on the y-axis, and the requirement of the problem is that \( \bar{orchestra}=rainstorm-waterlily \).

To find the centroid we introduce polar coordinates in the \( lemonade\text{-}orchestra \) plane. Corresponding to the element of area \( treasure d treasure d butterfly \) in the plane there is the element of volume
\[
2 \sqrt{waterlily^{2}-(treasure-rainstorm)^{2}}\, treasure \, d treasure \, d butterfly
\]
which contributes
\[
2\, treasure \sin butterfly \sqrt{waterlily^{2}-(treasure-rainstorm)^{2}}\, treasure \, d treasure \, d butterfly
\]
to the moment \( strawberry \) of the solid in the y direction: We have \( \bar{orchestra}=strawberry/pineapple \) where \( pineapple \) is the volume of the semi-toroid.
\[
\begin{aligned}
pineapple &= \int_{0}^{\pi} \int_{rainstorm-waterlily}^{rainstorm+waterlily} 2 \sqrt{waterlily^{2}}-\overline{(treasure-rainstorm)^{2}}\, treasure \, d treasure \, d butterfly \\ & = 2\pi \int_{rainstorm-waterlily}^{rainstorm+waterlily} \sqrt{waterlily^{2}-(treasure-\bar{rainstorm})^{2}}\, treasure \, d treasure \\ & = 2\pi waterlily^{2} \int_{-\pi/2}^{\pi/2} \cos harmony\,(rainstorm+waterlily \sin harmony) \cos harmony \, d harmony \\ & = \pi^{2} waterlily^{2} rainstorm . \\[6pt]
strawberry &= \int_{0}^{\pi} \int_{rainstorm-waterlily}^{rainstorm+waterlily} 2 \sqrt{waterlily^{2}-(treasure-rainstorm)^{2}}\, treasure^{2} \, d treasure \sin butterfly \, d butterfly \\ & = 4 \int_{rainstorm-waterlily}^{rainstorm+waterlily} \sqrt{waterlily^{2}-\frac{1}{(treasure-rainstorm)^{2}}}\, treasure^{2} \, d treasure \\ & = 4 waterlily^{2} \int_{-\pi/2}^{\pi/2} \cos harmony\,(rainstorm+waterlily \sin harmony)^{2} \cos harmony \, d harmony \\ & = 2\pi waterlily^{2} rainstorm^{2}+\frac{\pi}{2} waterlily^{4} .
\end{aligned}
\]

In both integrals we used the substitution \( treasure = rainstorm + waterlily \sin harmony \). Hence
\[
\bar{orchestra}=\frac{strawberry}{pineapple}=\frac{waterlily^{2}+4\, rainstorm^{2}}{2\pi rainstorm} .
\]

But we require \( \bar{orchestra}=rainstorm-waterlily \), so
\[
2\pi rainstorm^{2}-2\pi waterlily\, rainstorm = waterlily^{2}+4\, rainstorm^{2} .
\]

If \( dandelion = rainstorm / waterlily \), then
\[
(2\pi-4) dandelion^{2}-2\pi dandelion-1 = 0
\]
and
\[
dandelion = \frac{\pi+\sqrt{\pi^{2}+2\pi-4}}{2\pi-4} .
\]

We chose the positive sign since \( \boldsymbol{dandelion} \) must be positive.

Remarks. The volume of the semi-toroid could have been obtained from Pappus's Theorem that the volume of a solid of revolution is the product of the area of the generating region times the length of the circle traversed by the centroid of the area.

The critical ratio is about 2.91 .

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Problem:
<<<
5. A circle of radius \( centerpoint \) is revolved through \( 180^{\circ} \) about a line in its plane, distant \( nearshift \) from the center of the circle, where \( nearshift>centerpoint \). For what value of the ratio \( nearshift / centerpoint \) does the center of gravity of the solid thus generated lie on the surface of the solid?
>>>
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution:
<<<
Solution. We choose axes so that the generating circle starts in the \( verticalaxis-horizontalaxis \) plane and is revolved about the \( horizontalaxis \)-axis. The generated solid is half of a toroid (i.e., a solid bounded by a torus).

It is clear from symmetry that the centroid lies at a point \( (0, \bar{depthcoordinate}, 0) \) on the \( depthcoordinate \)-axis, and the requirement of the problem is that \( \bar{depthcoordinate}=nearshift-centerpoint \).

To find the centroid we introduce polar coordinates in the \( verticalaxis-depthcoordinate \) plane. Corresponding to the element of area \( angularmeasure d angularmeasure d linearoffset \) in the plane there is the element of volume
\[
2 \sqrt{centerpoint^{2}-(angularmeasure-nearshift)^{2}} angularmeasure d angularmeasure d linearoffset
\]
which contributes
\[
2 angularmeasure \sin linearoffset \sqrt{centerpoint^{2}-(angularmeasure-nearshift)^{2}} angularmeasure d angularmeasure d linearoffset
\]
to the moment \( restenergy \) of the solid in the \( depthcoordinate \) direction: We have \( \bar{depthcoordinate}=restenergy / hollowshell \) where \( hollowshell \) is the volume of the semi-toroid.
\[
\begin{aligned}
hollowshell & =\int_{0}^{\pi} \int_{nearshift-centerpoint}^{nearshift+centerpoint} 2 \sqrt{centerpoint^{2}}-\overline{(angularmeasure-nearshift)^{2}} angularmeasure d angularmeasure d linearoffset \\
& =2 \pi \int_{nearshift-centerpoint}^{nearshift+centerpoint} \sqrt{centerpoint^{2}-(angularmeasure-\bar{nearshift})^{2}} angularmeasure d angularmeasure \\
& =2 \pi centerpoint^{2} \int_{-\pi / 2}^{\pi / 2} \cos axialoffset(nearshift+centerpoint \sin axialoffset) \cos axialoffset d axialoffset \\
& =\pi^{2} centerpoint^{2} nearshift . \\
restenergy & =\int_{0}^{\pi} \int_{nearshift-centerpoint}^{nearshift+centerpoint} 2 \sqrt{centerpoint^{2}-(angularmeasure-nearshift)^{2}} angularmeasure^{2} d angularmeasure \sin linearoffset d linearoffset \\
& =4 \int_{nearshift-centerpoint}^{nearshift+centerpoint} \sqrt{centerpoint^{2}-\frac{1}{(angularmeasure-nearshift)^{2}} angularmeasure^{2} d angularmeasure} \\
& =4 centerpoint^{2} \int_{-\pi / 2}^{\pi / 2} \cos axialoffset(nearshift+centerpoint \sin axialoffset)^{2} \cos axialoffset d axialoffset \\
& =2 \pi centerpoint^{2} nearshift^{2}+\frac{\pi}{2} centerpoint^{4} .
\end{aligned}
\]

In both integrals we used the substitution \( angularmeasure=nearshift+centerpoint \sin axialoffset \).
Hence
\[
\bar{depthcoordinate}=\frac{restenergy}{hollowshell}=\frac{centerpoint^{2}+4 nearshift^{2}}{2 \pi nearshift} .
\]

But we require \( \bar{depthcoordinate}=nearshift-centerpoint \), so
\[
2 \pi nearshift^{2}-2 \pi centerpoint nearshift=centerpoint^{2}+4 nearshift^{2} .
\]

If \( summationfactor=nearshift / centerpoint \), then
\[
(2 \pi-4) summationfactor^{2}-2 \pi summationfactor-1=0
\]
and
\[
summationfactor=\frac{\pi+\sqrt{\pi^{2}+2 \pi-4}}{2 \pi-4} .
\]

We chose the positive sign since \( \boldsymbol{summationfactor} \) must be positive.
Remarks. The volume of the semi-toroid could have been obtained from Pappus's Theorem that the volume of a solid of revolution is the product of the area of the generating region times the length of the circle traversed by the centroid of the area.

The critical ratio is about 2.91 .
>>>

garbled_string.question: 5. A circle of radius \( klmnbvcx \) is revolved through \( 180^{\circ} \) about a line in its plane, distant \( sdfghjkl \) from the center of the circle, where \( sdfghjkl>klmnbvcx \). For what value of the ratio \( sdfghjkl / klmnbvcx \) does the center of gravity of the solid thus generated lie on the surface of the solid?
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. We choose axes so that the generating circle starts in the \( qzxwvtnp-nbvcyqwe \) plane and is revolved about the \( nbvcyqwe \)-axis. The generated solid is half of a toroid (i.e., a solid bounded by a torus).

It is clear from symmetry that the centroid lies at a point \( (0, \bar{hjgrksla}, 0) \) on the \( hjgrksla \)-axis, and the requirement of the problem is that \( \bar{hjgrksla}=sdfghjkl-klmnbvcx \).

To find the centroid we introduce polar coordinates in the \( qzxwvtnp-hjgrksla \) plane. Corresponding to the element of area \( ldkfjsie d ldkfjsie d pqowieur \) in the plane there is the element of volume
\[
2 \sqrt{klmnbvcx^{2}-(ldkfjsie-sdfghjkl)^{2}} ldkfjsie d ldkfjsie d pqowieur
\]
which contributes
\[
2 ldkfjsie \sin pqowieur \sqrt{klmnbvcx^{2}-(ldkfjsie-sdfghjkl)^{2}} ldkfjsie d ldkfjsie d pqowieur
\]
to the moment \( ghrtyuio \) of the solid in the \( hjgrksla \) direction: We have \( \bar{hjgrksla}=ghrtyuio / asdfghjk \) where \( asdfghjk \) is the volume of the semi-toroid.
\[
\begin{aligned}
asdfghjk & =\int_{0}^{\pi} \int_{sdfghjkl-klmnbvcx}^{sdfghjkl+klmnbvcx} 2 \sqrt{klmnbvcx^{2}}-\overline{(ldkfjsie-sdfghjkl)^{2}} ldkfjsie d ldkfjsie d pqowieur \\
& =2 \pi \int_{sdfghjkl-klmnbvcx}^{sdfghjkl+klmnbvcx} \sqrt{klmnbvcx^{2}-(ldkfjsie-\bar{sdfghjkl})^{2}} ldkfjsie d ldkfjsie \\
& =2 \pi klmnbvcx^{2} \int_{-\pi / 2}^{\pi / 2} \cos mxnbvzhr(sdfghjkl+klmnbvcx \sin mxnbvzhr) \cos mxnbvzhr d mxnbvzhr \\
& =\pi^{2} klmnbvcx^{2} sdfghjkl . \\
ghrtyuio & =\int_{0}^{\pi} \int_{sdfghjkl-klmnbvcx}^{sdfghjkl+klmnbvcx} 2 \sqrt{klmnbvcx^{2}-(ldkfjsie-sdfghjkl)^{2}} ldkfjsie^{2} d ldkfjsie \sin pqowieur d pqowieur \\
& =4 \int_{sdfghjkl-klmnbvcx}^{sdfghjkl+klmnbvcx} \sqrt{klmnbvcx^{2}-\frac{1}{(ldkfjsie-sdfghjkl)^{2}} ldkfjsie^{2} d ldkfjsie} \\
& =4 klmnbvcx^{2} \int_{-\pi / 2}^{\pi / 2} \cos mxnbvzhr(sdfghjkl+klmnbvcx \sin mxnbvzhr)^{2} \cos mxnbvzhr d mxnbvzhr \\
& =2 \pi klmnbvcx^{2} sdfghjkl^{2}+\frac{\pi}{2} klmnbvcx^{4} .
\end{aligned}
\]

In both integrals we used the substitution \( ldkfjsie=sdfghjkl+klmnbvcx \sin mxnbvzhr \).
Hence
\[
\bar{hjgrksla}=\frac{ghrtyuio}{asdfghjk}=\frac{klmnbvcx^{2}+4 sdfghjkl^{2}}{2 \pi sdfghjkl} .
\]

But we require \( \bar{hjgrksla}=sdfghjkl-klmnbvcx \), so
\[
2 \pi sdfghjkl^{2}-2 \pi klmnbvcx sdfghjkl=klmnbvcx^{2}+4 sdfghjkl^{2} .
\]

If \( zxcvbnml=sdfghjkl / klmnbvcx \), then
\[
(2 \pi-4) zxcvbnml^{2}-2 \pi zxcvbnml-1=0
\]
and
\[
zxcvbnml=\frac{\pi+\sqrt{\pi^{2}+2 \pi-4}}{2 \pi-4} .
\]

We chose the positive sign since \( \boldsymbol{zxcvbnml} \) must be positive.
Remarks. The volume of the semi-toroid could have been obtained from Pappus's Theorem that the volume of a solid of revolution is the product of the area of the generating region times the length of the circle traversed by the centroid of the area.

The critical ratio is about 2.91 .

kernel_variant.question:
Fix a radius $a>0$.  
In cylindrical coordinates $(\rho,\theta ,z)$ consider the circle  
\[
(\rho-d)^{2}+z^{2}=a^{2},\qquad d>a .
\]
Revolving this circle about the $z$-axis, but only through the angle $3\pi/2$,
\[
0\le\theta\le\frac{3\pi}{2},
\]
generates the three-quarter solid torus $T$ with
major (central) radius $d$ and tube radius $a$.

Inside $T$ the material is not homogeneous; its mass-density is prescribed by the power law  
\[
\varrho(\rho,\theta ,z)=\varrho_{0}\,\rho^{m},\qquad m\in\mathbb{N}\cup\{0\},\;
\varrho_{0}>0 .
\]

For which value(s) of the ratio  
\[
c:=\frac{d}{a}>1
\]
does the centre of mass $\,( \bar{\rho},\bar{\theta},\bar{z})\,$ of $T$ lie on the *outer* cylindrical surface
\[
\bar{\rho}=d+a=a(c+1)\;?
\]
(For symmetry reasons $\bar{\theta}=3\pi/4$ and $\bar{z}=0$, so only the radial coordinate $\bar{\rho}$ is unknown.)
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout let $c=d/a>1$ and $\varrho_{0}=1$ (it cancels in all ratios).

1.  Mass and first moment  
An infinitesimal mass element is
\[
dm=\rho^{m}\rho\,d\rho\,d\theta\,dz .
\]
For a fixed $\rho$ the circular cross-section satisfies
$|z|\le\sqrt{a^{2}-(\rho-d)^{2}}$; hence the corresponding slice of volume is
\[
2\sqrt{a^{2}-(\rho-d)^{2}}\;\rho\,d\rho\,d\theta .
\]
Introduce the family of integrals
\[
I_{k}(c):=\int_{d-a}^{d+a}\rho^{\,k}\;2\sqrt{a^{2}-(\rho-d)^{2}}\;d\rho ,
\qquad k\in\mathbb{N}.
\]
Because the density is independent of $\theta$ and $\theta$ ranges over
$3\pi/2$, we obtain
\[
M   =\frac{3\pi}{2}\,I_{m+1}(c),\qquad
M_{\rho}=\frac{3\pi}{2}\,I_{m+2}(c),
\]
so that
\[
\bar{\rho}=\frac{M_{\rho}}{M}=\frac{I_{m+2}(c)}{I_{m+1}(c)} .
\tag{1}
\]

2.  Trigonometric substitution  
Put $\rho=d+a\sin\varphi$ with $-\pi/2\le\varphi\le\pi/2$; then
$d\rho=a\cos\varphi\,d\varphi$ and
$\sqrt{a^{2}-(\rho-d)^{2}}=a\cos\varphi$.  Hence
\[
I_{k}(c)=2a^{k+2}\int_{-\pi/2}^{\pi/2}(c+\sin\varphi)^{k}(\cos\varphi)^{2}\,d\varphi .
\]
Write
\[
J_{k}(c):=\int_{-\pi/2}^{\pi/2}(c+\sin\varphi)^{k}(\cos\varphi)^{2}\,d\varphi ,
\qquad k\in\mathbb{N},
\]
so that by (1)
\[
\frac{\bar{\rho}}{a}=\frac{J_{m+2}(c)}{J_{m+1}(c)} .
\tag{2}
\]

3.  A combinatorial expansion  
Expand
$(c+\sin\varphi)^{k}$ via the binomial theorem and exploit evenness of $\sin\varphi$:
\[
(c+\sin\varphi)^{k}
=\sum_{j=0}^{\lfloor k/2\rfloor}\binom{k}{2j}c^{\,k-2j}\sin^{2j}\varphi .
\]
Consequently
\[
J_{k}(c)=\sum_{j=0}^{\lfloor k/2\rfloor}\binom{k}{2j}c^{\,k-2j}B_{j},
\qquad
B_{j}:=\int_{-\pi/2}^{\pi/2}\sin^{2j}\varphi(\cos\varphi)^{2}\,d\varphi .
\]
A standard Beta-function evaluation yields
\[
B_{j}=\frac{\pi(2j)!}{2^{2j+1}\,j!\,(j+1)!},\qquad j=0,1,2,\dots .
\tag{3}
\]

4.  Centroid condition  
Requiring $\bar{\rho}=a(c+1)$ is equivalent, via (2), to
\[
P_{m}(c):=J_{m+2}(c)-(c+1)J_{m+1}(c)=0 .
\tag{4}
\]
Insertion of the series for $J_{k}$ shows that $P_{m}(c)$ is a *polynomial*
of degree $m+2$ whose coefficients have mixed signs (already for $m=0$ one
finds $P_{0}(c)=\tfrac{\pi}{8}(1-4c)$).  
Only its *sign* for $c>1$ will matter, not the individual coefficients.

5.  An illuminating integral representation  
Returning to the defining integral one obtains directly
\[
P_{m}(c)=\int_{-\pi/2}^{\pi/2}
(c+\sin\varphi)^{\,m+1}\bigl(\sin\varphi-1\bigr)(\cos\varphi)^{2}\,d\varphi .
\tag{5}
\]
For $-\pi/2<\varphi<\pi/2$ we have $\cos\varphi>0$ and
$\sin\varphi<1$, so the factor $\sin\varphi-1$ is *strictly negative* on
a set of positive measure.  
Moreover $(c+\sin\varphi)^{\,m+1}>0$ for every $c>1$ and all $\varphi$ in the
domain.  Therefore
\[
P_{m}(c)<0\qquad\text{for every }c>1\text{ and every }m\in\mathbb{N}\cup\{0\}.
\tag{6}
\]

6.  Non-existence of admissible ratios  
Equation (4) can be satisfied only if $P_{m}(c)=0$, but (6) shows that
$P_{m}(c)$ is **strictly negative** throughout the physically relevant range
$c>1$.  Hence $P_{m}(c)$ possesses *no* root in this interval.  Consequently
\[
\boxed{\text{There is no ratio }c=d/a>1\text{ for which the centroid of the
three-quarter torus, endowed with the density }\rho^{m},\text{ lies on its
outer cylindrical surface.}}
\]

7.  Low-order checks (consistency)  
(i) $m=0$ (homogeneous material):
$P_{0}(c)=\tfrac{\pi}{8}(1-4c)$ has the single root $c=\tfrac14<1$.

(ii) $m=1$ (density $\propto\rho$):
$P_{1}(c)=\tfrac{\pi}{8}(-4c^{2}+2c-1)$ has negative discriminant, hence no real
root at all.

(iii) $m=2$ (density $\propto\rho^{2}$):
$P_{2}(c)=\tfrac{\pi}{16}(-8c^{3}+6c^{2}-6c+1)$ has the unique real root
$c\approx0.18<1$.

All examples confirm the general result.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
•  Higher technical level: the density is no longer constant but grows like ρ^m; the integrals therefore depend on an extra (unbounded) parameter m and lead to a whole *family* of algebraic equations instead of one quadratic.  
•  Algebra–analytic interplay: solving condition (5) required transforming volume-type integrals to Beta–function form, then converting the centroid condition into the polynomial (6).  
•  Advanced existence arguments: one has to analyse monotonicity of S_k and f_m to show uniqueness (or non-existence) of solutions for general m, not merely solve a single equation.  
•  Multiple interacting concepts: Beta/Gamma functions, binomial expansions, polynomial algebra, and qualitative root analysis all enter.  
•  Original solution was a single quadratic; the enhanced variant demands manipulating an infinite *sequence* of increasingly high-degree polynomials and understanding how their solvability depends on the parameter m.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Fix a radius $a>0$.  
In cylindrical coordinates $(\rho,\theta ,z)$ consider the circle  
\[
(\rho-d)^{2}+z^{2}=a^{2},\qquad d>a .
\]
Revolving this circle about the $z$-axis, but only through the angle $3\pi/2$,
\[
0\le\theta\le\frac{3\pi}{2},
\]
generates the three-quarter solid torus $T$ with
major (central) radius $d$ and tube radius $a$.

Inside $T$ the material is not homogeneous; its mass-density is prescribed by the power law  
\[
\varrho(\rho,\theta ,z)=\varrho_{0}\,\rho^{m},\qquad m\in\mathbb{N}\cup\{0\},\;
\varrho_{0}>0 .
\]

For which value(s) of the ratio  
\[
c:=\frac{d}{a}>1
\]
does the centre of mass $\,( \bar{\rho},\bar{\theta},\bar{z})\,$ of $T$ lie on the *outer* cylindrical surface
\[
\bar{\rho}=d+a=a(c+1)\;?
\]
(For symmetry reasons $\bar{\theta}=3\pi/4$ and $\bar{z}=0$, so only the radial coordinate $\bar{\rho}$ is unknown.)
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout let $c=d/a>1$ and $\varrho_{0}=1$ (it cancels in all ratios).

1.  Mass and first moment  
An infinitesimal mass element is
\[
dm=\rho^{m}\rho\,d\rho\,d\theta\,dz .
\]
For a fixed $\rho$ the circular cross-section satisfies
$|z|\le\sqrt{a^{2}-(\rho-d)^{2}}$; hence the corresponding slice of volume is
\[
2\sqrt{a^{2}-(\rho-d)^{2}}\;\rho\,d\rho\,d\theta .
\]
Introduce the family of integrals
\[
I_{k}(c):=\int_{d-a}^{d+a}\rho^{\,k}\;2\sqrt{a^{2}-(\rho-d)^{2}}\;d\rho ,
\qquad k\in\mathbb{N}.
\]
Because the density is independent of $\theta$ and $\theta$ ranges over
$3\pi/2$, we obtain
\[
M   =\frac{3\pi}{2}\,I_{m+1}(c),\qquad
M_{\rho}=\frac{3\pi}{2}\,I_{m+2}(c),
\]
so that
\[
\bar{\rho}=\frac{M_{\rho}}{M}=\frac{I_{m+2}(c)}{I_{m+1}(c)} .
\tag{1}
\]

2.  Trigonometric substitution  
Put $\rho=d+a\sin\varphi$ with $-\pi/2\le\varphi\le\pi/2$; then
$d\rho=a\cos\varphi\,d\varphi$ and
$\sqrt{a^{2}-(\rho-d)^{2}}=a\cos\varphi$.  Hence
\[
I_{k}(c)=2a^{k+2}\int_{-\pi/2}^{\pi/2}(c+\sin\varphi)^{k}(\cos\varphi)^{2}\,d\varphi .
\]
Write
\[
J_{k}(c):=\int_{-\pi/2}^{\pi/2}(c+\sin\varphi)^{k}(\cos\varphi)^{2}\,d\varphi ,
\qquad k\in\mathbb{N},
\]
so that by (1)
\[
\frac{\bar{\rho}}{a}=\frac{J_{m+2}(c)}{J_{m+1}(c)} .
\tag{2}
\]

3.  A combinatorial expansion  
Expand
$(c+\sin\varphi)^{k}$ via the binomial theorem and exploit evenness of $\sin\varphi$:
\[
(c+\sin\varphi)^{k}
=\sum_{j=0}^{\lfloor k/2\rfloor}\binom{k}{2j}c^{\,k-2j}\sin^{2j}\varphi .
\]
Consequently
\[
J_{k}(c)=\sum_{j=0}^{\lfloor k/2\rfloor}\binom{k}{2j}c^{\,k-2j}B_{j},
\qquad
B_{j}:=\int_{-\pi/2}^{\pi/2}\sin^{2j}\varphi(\cos\varphi)^{2}\,d\varphi .
\]
A standard Beta-function evaluation yields
\[
B_{j}=\frac{\pi(2j)!}{2^{2j+1}\,j!\,(j+1)!},\qquad j=0,1,2,\dots .
\tag{3}
\]

4.  Centroid condition  
Requiring $\bar{\rho}=a(c+1)$ is equivalent, via (2), to
\[
P_{m}(c):=J_{m+2}(c)-(c+1)J_{m+1}(c)=0 .
\tag{4}
\]
Insertion of the series for $J_{k}$ shows that $P_{m}(c)$ is a *polynomial*
of degree $m+2$ whose coefficients have mixed signs (already for $m=0$ one
finds $P_{0}(c)=\tfrac{\pi}{8}(1-4c)$).  
Only its *sign* for $c>1$ will matter, not the individual coefficients.

5.  An illuminating integral representation  
Returning to the defining integral one obtains directly
\[
P_{m}(c)=\int_{-\pi/2}^{\pi/2}
(c+\sin\varphi)^{\,m+1}\bigl(\sin\varphi-1\bigr)(\cos\varphi)^{2}\,d\varphi .
\tag{5}
\]
For $-\pi/2<\varphi<\pi/2$ we have $\cos\varphi>0$ and
$\sin\varphi<1$, so the factor $\sin\varphi-1$ is *strictly negative* on
a set of positive measure.  
Moreover $(c+\sin\varphi)^{\,m+1}>0$ for every $c>1$ and all $\varphi$ in the
domain.  Therefore
\[
P_{m}(c)<0\qquad\text{for every }c>1\text{ and every }m\in\mathbb{N}\cup\{0\}.
\tag{6}
\]

6.  Non-existence of admissible ratios  
Equation (4) can be satisfied only if $P_{m}(c)=0$, but (6) shows that
$P_{m}(c)$ is **strictly negative** throughout the physically relevant range
$c>1$.  Hence $P_{m}(c)$ possesses *no* root in this interval.  Consequently
\[
\boxed{\text{There is no ratio }c=d/a>1\text{ for which the centroid of the
three-quarter torus, endowed with the density }\rho^{m},\text{ lies on its
outer cylindrical surface.}}
\]

7.  Low-order checks (consistency)  
(i) $m=0$ (homogeneous material):
$P_{0}(c)=\tfrac{\pi}{8}(1-4c)$ has the single root $c=\tfrac14<1$.

(ii) $m=1$ (density $\propto\rho$):
$P_{1}(c)=\tfrac{\pi}{8}(-4c^{2}+2c-1)$ has negative discriminant, hence no real
root at all.

(iii) $m=2$ (density $\propto\rho^{2}$):
$P_{2}(c)=\tfrac{\pi}{16}(-8c^{3}+6c^{2}-6c+1)$ has the unique real root
$c\approx0.18<1$.

All examples confirm the general result.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
•  Higher technical level: the density is no longer constant but grows like ρ^m; the integrals therefore depend on an extra (unbounded) parameter m and lead to a whole *family* of algebraic equations instead of one quadratic.  
•  Algebra–analytic interplay: solving condition (5) required transforming volume-type integrals to Beta–function form, then converting the centroid condition into the polynomial (6).  
•  Advanced existence arguments: one has to analyse monotonicity of S_k and f_m to show uniqueness (or non-existence) of solutions for general m, not merely solve a single equation.  
•  Multiple interacting concepts: Beta/Gamma functions, binomial expansions, polynomial algebra, and qualitative root analysis all enter.  
•  Original solution was a single quadratic; the enhanced variant demands manipulating an infinite *sequence* of increasingly high-degree polynomials and understanding how their solvability depends on the parameter m.
